headline: Ancient Fish Make a Comeback in Shared River
meta_description: Chinese and Russian biologists release nearly 500,000 young sturgeon into the Amur River, marking a turning point in cross-border conservation.
summary_short: After decades of overfishing nearly wiped them out, sturgeon are returning to the Amur River thanks to coordinated efforts between China and Russia.
body:
In 2008, the BBC traveled to China's Heilongjiang province searching for the river's legendary giant sturgeon—only to discover there were none left. The Black Dragon River, as it's known locally, had been chronically overfished.

Now, more than fifteen years later, something remarkable is happening. On the first Tuesday in July, environmental authorities from China and Russia gathered in Tongjiang city to release nearly 500,000 young sturgeon into the shared waterway they call the Amur River.

The release included 200 juvenile Amur sturgeon and 485,000 juvenile kaluga, another sturgeon species. Scientists marked many of the fish with fluorescent dye to track their movements and reproduction patterns throughout their lives.

The most encouraging sign: monitored kaluga and Amur sturgeon are now surviving to five years and older—something unrecorded as recently as 2017. In 2025, researchers documented a sturgeon reaching eight years old, the age when males first contribute to spawning.

"I have been fishing for decades," said Li Changyou, a local fisherman. "Now we have fish release activities every year, and seeing bags of fry being released into the river makes me truly happy."

While past recovery efforts produced mixed results, this collaborative approach offers genuine hope—and a livelihood restored.
